route: Followed the unmaintained trail up past Sangrees Hut.
description: Witnessed the after of an avalanche that occurred in a 35 to 40 degree area. High winds with warming.

weather_detail.description: A beautiful spring-like day with light to moderate northwest winds at ridgelines.

snowpack_detail.description:
We dug on two aspects around the near treeline band—one on a southeast-facing slope around 11,800ft. The snow height was 76cm with a mix of melt-freeze crusts and weak facets in the bottom 50cm of the snowpack. Our Extended Column Test produced propagating results at the top of the facet layer on two tests. One failed on isolation, and the other was ECTP24. Of note here is that the entire snowpack is moist to wet. There is evidence of water moving through the entire snowpack with percolation tubes throughout the weak facet layer.

Our second pit was on an east-facing slope around 12,000ft. We saw dry snow throughout the entire 88cm of snowpack. Here, there was a more cohesive slab roughly 50cm thick that rested on large basal depth hoar. Our ECTs here produced propagation on hard results (ECTP 21 and 25). It appeared that this layer failed in the middle of the facet layer, but upon closer examination, it failed just below a decomposing Pencil minus melt-freeze crust.

Of note here is that the entire snowpack is moist to wet. There is evidence of water moving through the entire snowpack with percolation tubes throughout the weak facet layer.

assets.caption: Southeast-facing slopes near treeline have moist to wet snow throughout the snowpack. There is evidence of snow moving all the way to the ground.
assets.alternate_text: This snowpit profile, recorded by Austin DiVesta on March 12, 2026, at Deadwood Gulch (San Juan Range, CO), illustrates a shallow and unstable snowpack totaling 76 cm on a 28° Southeast-facing slope

assets.caption: Snow coverage on the Grand Turk and around Sultan on more north-facing slopes.

East-facing slopes around 12,000ft remain dry and not a spring-like snowpack. While Extended Column Tests results are getting harder to trigger, you might still find the shallow spot and trigger a large avalanche.

More north-facing slopes near and above treeline are a mixed bag, with trap-door stiff snow on top of soft snow below or stiff hollow-sounding snow. These are places that you can still trigger a Persistent Slab avalanche.
South-facing slopes are patchy or back down to the rocks.
Snow coverage on east-facing terrain near and above treeline on the the King Mine Slide path on Grand Turk.
A graphed snow profile on an East-facing aspect at ~12,000 ft.
assets.alternate_text: A documented snow profile on an east-facing slope around 12,000ft.

objective: Gauging near-mid term wet issues on easterly-facing terrain
description: The warm weather continues, but just because the calendar says we are in meteorological spring does not mean our snowpack is spring-like yet. While some of the below treeline slopes were more of obstacle dodging than skiing, there was still cold snow on northerly-facing slopes. Any slope that receives a bit more sun has a thin crust at the surface. We did note that around 10:15 AM, the snow began to soften a bit on more of those easterly-facing slopes. As we moved into the near treeline band, we noticed a mixed bag of conditions, including soft wind-textured snow and both wind and sun crusts on the surface. Slopes that face on the north side of the compass in the alpine looked fairly wind-hammered with varied conditions.

assets.caption: A recent avalanche near Whitehouse Mountain. Image taken March 12, 2026.
Some crown faces are still visible from a few very large (D3 or larger) avalanches off Treasure Mountain that slid around February 18.  Image taken March 12, 2026.

weather_detail.description: Moderate snow transport from the northwest.

snowpack_detail.description:
Yesterday's snowfall has already begun to be affected by the sun and wind, with a sun crust developing on southerly aspects and a wind skin on east aspects. These were thin, and skis could punch through, but it certainly made for some variable skiing. Where you found the tiger strip, the skiing was very pleasant.

We dug on northeast and southeast aspects, and the structure was somewhat similar. The two weak layers of concern were the February facets and the basal facets. On sunnier aspects, these are separated by a variety of crusts and stronger layers. The basal facets were moist on a southeast aspect, and we were able to make a snowball from them. When digging this layer, it felt very weak, but it is starting to gain strength and was 1 finger to 4 finger hard. Continued warm weather should work on this further, and we should soon be able to remove southeast from the PSa rose.

We dug on a shallow and weak northeast slope that was only about 80cm deep. At this location, the February facets were only about 30cm deep, and the slab above was weak and broke during a Propagation Saw Test. We did get propagation on the basal facets, showing the potential for avalanches to step down to the ground in weaker snowpack areas.

assets.caption: In this shallow and weak snowpack area it is easy to see the two weak layers. February facets at 60cm and a basal weak layer at 25 cm. The slab in this area was weak but in other areas we could probe very stout layers between the weak snow,
On this southeast aspect the layer of failure was facets at 30cm.

route: We toured up Straight Creek to the end and returned the same way. We traveled southeast through northeast aspects and looked at lots of stripped westerlies.
objective: Look at SE for the PSa rose. Look at overall sensitivity.
description: The snowpack was quiet today, and although we dug into very shallow, weak slopes, the snow was not super reactive. It did show what is still possible if you find the wrong area of the slope, but in this area it felt like we are moving towards a low likelihood, high consequence scenario where multiple people could ride a slope before it is triggered from a weaker area of the slope.

route: Toured from Loveland Pass to Pacific Gully. Booted back up Pacific gully and skied Old Reliable.
description:
Some minor cracking and evidence of a windslab approximately 12in thick on a crossloaded SE aspect. Evidence of significant wind loading on N through S aspects. Wind lighter than forecast on Loveland Pass today. Cloudy and cool, no wet snow above treeline.

12000' SW Aspect. ECTX. Melt-freeze crust beneath the recent storm snow. Did not find any significant persistent weak layer on our slope.

snowpack_detail.description: On northeast facing terrain, there was an average height of snow between 60-85cm. I found one isolated roll over with a height of snow closer to 120cm. There are two melt freeze crust sitting above a pencil hard slab (see snow profile below). Snowpack tests on this aspect resulted in propagation in deep faceted layers. It is worth keeping an eye on this poor structure for wet avalanche issues as warming continues through the week.

On northeast facing terrain, there was an average height of snow between 60-85cm. I found one isolated roll over with a height of snow closer to 120cm. There are two melt freeze crust sitting above a pencil hard slab (see snow profile below). Snowpack tests on this aspect resulted in propagation in deep faceted layers. It is worth keeping an eye on this poor structure for wet avalanche issues as warming continues through the week.

route: Follow road then ascend up northeast and east facing terrain up to 10,500 ft.
description: There is not much snow coverage below 8,500 ft. East facing slopes had patchy to zero coverage up to 10,500 ft. Where there is snow coverage, the upper part of the snowpack is made up of melt freeze crusts sitting above a pencil hard slab. As it got later in the day the crusts sitting on the snow surface slowly became shiny and soft, leaving my skins gloppy and wet. I saw no signs of instability below tree line.

assets.caption: Public Observation: Kebler Pass

description: Breezy the entire time. Corn on steep S/SE pitches at noonish, turned into firm but fair conditions once slope angle decreased. No signs of instability. Soft snow in trees on NE aspects. Some evidence still of rain crust 3 inches down but not supportive and could ski through it without much issue.

weather_detail.description: Sunny day with a few clouds in the sky. It was fairly cold this morning, just below 0 in the valley floor. A little bit breezy, and it looks like it's much windier at higher elevations. A couple of inches of new low-density snow.

snowpack_detail.description: North aspects at the upper end of below treeline are still concerning and have a winter snowpack. The warming has affected sunny aspects, wrapping around to east and creeping into northeast. These new stout crusts and percolation will greatly reduce the Persistent Slab problem.

North aspects at the upper end of below treeline are still concerning and have a winter snowpack. The warming has affected sunny aspects, wrapping around to east and creeping into northeast. These new stout crusts and percolation will greatly reduce the Persistent Slab problem.

assets.caption: ECTP 21 test displaying a 70 cm deep weak facet layer underneath a thick, cohesive slab on a north-facing slope at 11200'.
The snowpack on north slopes is still cold and dry and has not experienced any water moving through.
An east-facing surface at 11,100 feet elevation has a stout crust with a one-finger mid-pack slab. Percolation can be observed throughout the whole snowpack down to the mid-pack.
Percolation tubes are burrowing through a thick slab, leading downwards into the middle of the pack.

area: East, Northeast, North, and West facing slopes from 10,500 feet to 11,100 feet, including areas below treeline and the upper end of below treeline. Also, a ridge above Gore Creek.
route: Traveled up Gore Creek Normal Trailhead to the Ridge above the Creek.
objective: Assess ongoing PSa concerns and water movement in the snowpack
description:
On north-facing slopes, triggering an avalanche is becoming more difficult. However, if one were triggered, it could still be large due to a concerning, persistent slab structure that remains intact. The snowpack is still a winter's snowpack, with a 70cn slab sitting on dry. Conversely, a stout crust layer extends from east to northeast aspects before disappearing on true north aspects and has started to transition, as on other sunnier aspects.

On east-facing slopes around 11,100 feet, there is a strong surface crust over a mid-pack slab. Meltwater has percolated down into the mid-pack, and the old snow surface is moist to wet. Beneath this layer are hard crusts, indicating that these slopes have warmed and refrozen. It would be very difficult to impact layers below these crusts. Overall, the snowpack here is transitioning toward a spring snowpack structure.

On a west-facing slope at 10,500 feet below treeline, the snowpack is largely refrozen. Meltwater has percolated through much of the snowpack, and most layers have refrozen. The base remains moist, while the upper portion has formed a thick, strong crust that is not currently a concern.

avalanche_observations.comments: Small loose wet avalanches running when cold, new snow saw the sun for the first time.
avalanche_observations.area: Below Bear Peak, below cornice, sunny slope

avalanche_detail.description: I saw about 4 small cornice fall avalanches on very steep east-facing slopes above treeline near the summit of Glacier Mountain. One of these broke as a Wind Slab avalanche, but, surprisingly, none triggered deeper layers. Additionally, I saw multiple small Loose Dry avalanches from cornice fall near Bear Peak this morning. An older, large (D2) Persistent Slab avalanche, likely from last weekend, was observed below Little Bear Peak. It was possibly triggered by Loose Wet avalanches from above, with the crown in the middle of the path, well below the ridge.

avalanche_observations.comments: Cornice triggered a very small windslab avalanche; surprisingly did not trigger anything deeper.

avalanche_observations.comments: Older persistent slab avalanche, D2, likely ran last Sunday. Triggered by loose wet from above with a mid-path crown. Big enough to kill a person.

avalanche_observations.comments: Observed 5 natural cornice fall avalanches, D1, with large pieces of cornice (as big as a small sedan). Most were debris, one triggered a wind slab. Did not trigger anything deeper.
avalanche_observations.area: Summit of Glacier Peak, steep E facing slope

weather_detail.description: There was about 4 inches of new snow and moderate to strong northwest winds with broken skies and temperatures in the 10s and 20s.

snowpack_detail.description: The new snow was forming a soft slab just below ridgetop, about four fingers on the Hand Hardness Scale. I got some minor cracking and propagation after a single tap in an Extended Column Test (ECT), but there was surprisingly little Wind Slab avalanche activity. It looked like a number of sluffs were triggered from cornicefall right when the wind picked up. I got harder propagating results deeper in the snowpack in all of my ECTs, the depth varied, but the results were all at the interface with the basal facet layer. There were also mid-pack weak layers, although they weren't reactive in my snowpits. Again, this was surprising since most of the recent avalanches broke on these layers before stepping down.

I got harder propagating results deeper in the snowpack in all of my ECTs, the depth varied, but the results were all at the interface with the basal facet layer. assets.caption: A long, yet relatively small, avalanche is seen running down the steep east face of Glacier Mountain thanks to a natural cornice fall from the ridge.
A close-up image of the debris below the ridge of Glacier Mountain.
A small slab of newly drifted snow breaking within a layer of softer snow from a small cornice drop on a test slope.
If you look closely, you can see where the cornice fall triggered a small Wind Slab avalanche,
View of an old avalanche on Little Bear Mountain near Sts. John, possibly triggered by loose wet snow sluffing from above.
A close-up image of the large avalanche below Little Bear Mountain near Sts. John. You can see where the avalanche broke across the whole slope below the cliffs in the middle of the path.
Small Loose Dry sluffs below the ridge of Bear Mountain, thanks to cornice fall after the wind increased.
A profile of my snowpit on an east-facing near treeline slope.

area: Glacier Mountain near Montezuma in the Front Range
route: I went from the Deer Creek trailhead, following old mining roads and skin tracks, to the summit of Glacier Mountain. Descended the east face and followed the ascent route back to the truck.
objective: Assess the sensitivity of PSa and the thickness/density of the slab on E/NE. Look for new WSa and recent avalanches.
description: With about 4 inches of new snow and strong northwest winds, new slabs were developing on leeward slopes. I observed several small Wind Slab and Loose Dry avalanches from this morning, triggered by cornice falls that, surprisingly, did not trigger deeper layers. I expect the new slabs to lose sensitivity relatively quickly. I saw some minor cracking in the new slab, and it was reactive in my snowpit tests, but I didn't notice it breaking on a persistent grain. The main concern is still triggering something that breaks two or three feet deep, widely across the terrain, growing large enough to bury you. I could see a slide like this across the valley below Little Bear Mountain that likely ran last Sunday. On below treeline easterly-facing test slopes, I got one slab to crack, but it did not slide. You can probably still find a problematic slab, especially at the upper end of the below treeline elevation band, but it's not as likely as near and above treeline. That said, I still decided to avoid a steep, loaded, north-facing slope below treeline.
